rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative 1,2-migration of α,α-disubstituted allyl alcohols to α-exo-methylene ketones using calcium or magnesium halide salts as halogen mediators
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of halide (Cl- or Br-) from CaX2/MgX2 generates an electrophilic halogen species (X+) that activates the allyl alcohol; cathode reduces MeOH to H2 and methoxide

key_intermediates.name: electrophilic halogen species (X+) generated at anode from halide mediator
halonium ion / halogenated alkoxide adduct of allyl alcohol (X added across C=C with adjacent O-H)
α-halo ketone (after 1,2-migration and loss of H+)
α-exo-methylene ketone (final product after base-mediated dehydrohalogenation)

electron_flow: Halide (from CaX2 or MgX2) is oxidized at the anode to an electrophilic X+ species, which adds to the allyl alcohol alkene; 1,2-migration with loss of H+ delivers an α-halo ketone; cathodic reduction of MeOH produces methoxide which (or added base) effects dehydrohalogenation to the α-exo-methylene ketone.
notes: Mechanism is proposed in Scheme 1 without direct mechanistic probes shown on these pages; halide-mediator role inferred from screening of supporting electrolytes (Et4NBF4 inactive; chloride/bromide salts active). Calcium and magnesium halides outperform alkali metal chlorides, and mixed MeOH/MeCN solvent suppresses electrode fouling from insoluble Ca species.
